actual_meaning: what's the plan / what's going on / what's the situation here
note: English 'scene' is borrowed and recoded as 'situation/plan'; Hindi 'kya scene hai' survives but a Tamil dub would need 'enna nadakkudhu' or 'enna plan' and loses the casual swagger of the borrowed word.

actual_meaning: what's up / what's the deal
note: Greeting-cum-enquiry. Word-for-word into either language reads as a film term, not a casual 'sup'; needs idiomatic substitution.

actual_meaning: everything's arranged / we're all set / the situation is sorted
note: Two English loanwords doing idiomatic work. A literal Hindi/Tamil rendering loses it entirely; needs 'sab set hai' / 'ellam ready' style rewrite.

actual_meaning: something's happening / the party/action is live
note: Slangy 'on' as 'active'. No clean equivalent; dub must paraphrase as 'mazaa chal raha hai' or similar.

actual_meaning: no problem / no big deal / it's chill
note: Reassurance idiom. Tamil 'onnum problem illa' works in sense but the breezy 'scene' tone is lost in both targets.

actual_meaning: a way to kill time / something casual and unserious / mediocre
note: Quintessential Indian-English coinage with no single-word equivalent; can be noun, verb or dismissive adjective ('movie was timepass'). Tamil/Hindi both just borrow it.

actual_meaning: just killing time / loafing around
note: Verbed loanword. A literal Tamil dub has no native verb for this casual idling sense; usually keeps 'timepass'.

actual_meaning: a clever improvised fix / hack / workaround using limited resources
note: Culturally loaded, near-untranslatable concept. English 'hack/workaround' undershoots; Tamil has no native single word, often borrows 'jugaad' or paraphrases.

actual_meaning: a resourceful person who improvises fixes
note: Adjective/noun form of jugaad. No equivalent in Tamil; even Hindi-to-Tamil dub keeps the word as-is.

actual_meaning: pull some strings / improvise a fix / arrange it somehow
note: Collocation 'jugaad lagana' = to wangle/finagle. Whole idiom needs rewrite in Tamil; literal gloss is meaningless.

actual_meaning: an arrangement / a fix / an inside deal or romantic involvement
note: Polysemous slang: a bribe-arrangement, an understanding, or a couple's affair. English 'setting' means none of these; both Hindi and Tamil use it as slang loan.

actual_meaning: the deal/arrangement is sorted / it's been fixed (often via influence)
note: Implies a quiet fix or romance has clicked. Literal translation loses the conspiratorial wink; Tamil dub paraphrases as 'set aagiduchu'.

actual_meaning: to arrange/fix something through connections or bribery
note: Verb collocation with corrupt/underhand undertone. No direct equivalent; needs context-specific rewrite in both languages.

actual_meaning: a chance / an opening / potential (often romantic)
note: 'Scope hai kya' = is there a chance. Diverges from English 'scope'; Tamil keeps the loanword, literal Hindi sense doesn't carry the flirty 'chance' meaning.

actual_meaning: no chance / it's not going to happen
note: Dismissive 'no opening'. English literal misses it; both target dubs need 'chance illa / koi chance nahi' rewrite.

actual_meaning: money / cash on hand
note: Used loosely for personal cash ('fund hai?'), unlike formal English 'funds'. Tamil/Hindi both borrow casually; literal sense is too formal.

actual_meaning: completely / intensely / totally (intensifier)
note: Intensifier loaned into both languages ('full-on filmy'). Hindi 'poori tarah' / Tamil 'romba' approximate but lose the youthful punch.

actual_meaning: totally / fully (slangy intensifier, esp. Mumbai)
note: Tapori-flavoured intensifier ('full to paisa vasool'). Regionally marked; no equivalent, Tamil would substitute 'fulla'.

actual_meaning: totally / exactly / absolutely (intensifier or agreement)
note: Versatile Hindi intensifier; 'ekdum sahi' = spot on. Tamil dub uses 'sema/correct-a' but the snap of 'ekdum' as a standalone agreement is lost.

actual_meaning: all of a sudden / abruptly
note: Distinct from bare 'ekdum'; means suddenly. Tamil 'thideer-nu' covers it but timing/register differs in lip-sync.

actual_meaning: I mean / like / so basically (discourse filler)
note: Started as 'meaning' but works as a hesitation filler. Literal 'meaning' is wrong in filler use; Tamil dub uses 'adhaan' or drops it.

actual_meaning: like, that's ridiculous / come on, anything goes now
note: Exclamatory disbelief idiom. Word-for-word is nonsense; needs full rewrite to convey the eye-roll in both targets.

actual_meaning: essentially / so / in short (overused Indian-English filler)
note: English loan filler peppering Indian speech. Has equivalents (Hindi 'basically/asal mein', Tamil 'basically/moolama') but its filler frequency is hard to match naturally in dub.

actual_meaning: right? / isn't it? (tag question) or softener
note: Tag particle ('chalein na?'). Hindi keeps 'na'; Tamil uses 'illa/la'. Carries emotional softening that a flat tag doesn't reproduce; tricky for lip-sync because it's tiny.

actual_meaning: no no / wait, not like that (mild protest)
note: Reduplicated negation. Mostly translates ('illa illa' in Tamil) but rhythm and tone need care.

actual_meaning: dude / man / buddy (filler of familiarity or exasperation)
note: More a tonal filler than 'friend'. Tamil 'da/machan', Hindi keeps 'yaar'. The emotional colour (whining, affection, frustration) shifts with each substitute.

actual_meaning: ugh I mean / dude, like... (frustrated hesitation combo)
note: Stacked fillers conveying exasperated searching for words. No literal equivalent; dub must improvise natural filler stacking.

actual_meaning: oh! / hey! / come on! (exclamation of surprise, protest or attention)
note: Multi-emotion interjection. Tamil 'aiyo/dei' covers parts but not all shades; meaning is entirely tone-dependent, so dub timing matters more than the word.

actual_meaning: oh come on / ugh man (mild exasperation)
note: Fixed exasperation combo. Tamil 'aiyo da' is the nearest but loses the exact register; Hindi keeps it.

actual_meaning: oh wonderful! / well done! (genuine or sarcastic praise)
note: Praise interjection that flips sarcastic by tone. Tamil 'super-a' or 'aha' approximate; the sarcasm layer is hard to dub.

actual_meaning: oi! / hey you! (rough, familiar or aggressive address)
note: Low-register, slightly rude vocative. Tamil 'dei' is close; English 'hey' is too neutral. Register loss is the dub risk.

actual_meaning: oi you idiot / hey rascal (rough affectionate or abusive address)
note: 'Saale' is a mild abuse-cum-endearment; literal 'brother-in-law' is meaningless. Tamil 'dei loosu' style needed; censorship/register a dubbing headache.

actual_meaning: hey! / oi! (call for attention, often Punjabi-flavoured)
note: Attention-grabbing interjection. Tamil 'dei/ey' works but the Punjabi warmth of 'oye' is lost in a Tamil dub.

actual_meaning: ooh la la / look at you (teasing admiration)
note: Teasing exclamation, often when someone dresses up or flirts. No equivalent; Tamil dub must coin a playful interjection.

actual_meaning: so anyway / right then / so as I was saying (resumption marker)
note: Discourse resumption filler. Literal 'yes so' is clumsy; Tamil 'apparam/seri' resumes but the casual flow differs.

actual_meaning: so / then (discourse connective and emphatic particle)
note: Tiny but does heavy emphatic work ('main toh jaa raha hu'). Tamil 'naan-aanaa' style emphasis or particle 'thaan'; never a clean one-to-one map.

actual_meaning: that'll do / it works / acceptable
note: Idiomatic 'good enough'. Literal 'it will walk' is wrong; Tamil 'pordhu/aagum' captures sense but loses the shrug-acceptance flavour.

actual_meaning: it'll work out / it'll be fine / we can manage with it
note: Reassuring 'it'll do'. Literal motion verb misleads; Tamil 'aaidum/pordhu' needed with a casual tone.

actual_meaning: it's okay / these things happen / we let it slide (resigned acceptance)
note: Famous 'chalta hai' attitude of casual tolerance. Literal is meaningless; conveys a whole cultural mindset hard to compress in dub.

actual_meaning: it'll get done / don't worry, it'll happen (often vague reassurance)
note: Classic non-committal reassurance. Tamil 'aaidum' is close in words but the breezy 'no fixed timeline' undertone is cultural and easy to lose.

actual_meaning: done / it's finished / sorted
note: Fairly translatable ('mudinjuduchu' in Tamil) but used as a one-word filler-announcement; lip-sync of the clipped delivery is the challenge.

actual_meaning: we'll see / let's wait and watch (often a polite non-commitment)
note: Frequently a soft no/deferral, not a literal 'we will see'. Tamil 'paappom' carries it but the diplomatic dodge is register-sensitive.

actual_meaning: we'll deal with it later / cross that bridge when we get there
note: Idiomatic procrastination. Literal passive 'it will be seen' fails; Tamil needs 'apparam paathukalaam' rewrite.

actual_meaning: your current spot / where you are (as in 'send location' or 'kya location hai')
note: Loanword now also slang for 'where are you'. Tamil/Hindi both borrow it; mostly translatable but the casual 'loc bhej' clipping is colloquial.

actual_meaning: a regular hangout spot / den
note: Culturally rich word for a habitual gathering place. English 'hangout' undershoots; Tamil 'adda' is itself borrowed in many contexts.

actual_meaning: chill / take it easy / don't take it seriously
note: Pure slang from 'take it lightly'. Literal 'take light' is meaningless; Tamil 'cool-a iru' / 'light-a edu' (also borrowed) needed.

actual_meaning: took it lightly / didn't take it seriously / brushed it off
note: Past form of 'light lena'. Idiom won't survive literal gloss; Tamil dub usually keeps 'light' as a loan verb.

actual_meaning: it's chill / no big deal / relax
note: Slang reassurance. Literal 'it is light' (weight/lamp) confuses; needs 'cool-a iruku' rewrite in Tamil.

actual_meaning: a glitch / shady mess / something fishy or a clever hack
note: Slang for a flaw, scam or sneaky trick depending on context. No equivalent; Tamil dub must pick 'gola' or paraphrase per context.

actual_meaning: something's fishy / there's a catch / it's messed up
note: Suspicion idiom. Literal nonsensical; Tamil 'edho gola iruku' captures it but with different imagery.

actual_meaning: trouble / a messy quarrel or complication (often Mumbai slang)
note: Tapori word for a brawl/entanglement, sometimes a love affair. No equivalent; Tamil 'gola/problem' approximates loosely.

actual_meaning: trouble broke out / things got messy
note: Whole idiom needs rewrite; Tamil 'problem aaiduchu' loses the streetwise Mumbai colour.

actual_meaning: trouble picked deliberately / a beef / a risky confrontation
note: Punjabi/Hindi slang; 'panga lena' = to mess with someone. No native Tamil equivalent; dub borrows or rewrites as 'mokka/scene-podu'.

actual_meaning: don't mess with (me/them) / don't pick a fight
note: Warning idiom. Literal gloss fails entirely; Tamil 'scene podaadhe' style rewrite required.

actual_meaning: sorted it / arranged it / settled it (sometimes 'dealt with' a person)
note: English 'fix' + Hindi verb. Ambiguous between 'arranged' and ominous 'took care of him'. Tamil 'fix panniten' borrows the same loan; tone carries meaning.

actual_meaning: arranged it / sorted it / set it up
note: 'Set' as loan verb for arranging. Tamil 'set panniten' parallels; literal native-language verb loses the casual borrowed feel.

actual_meaning: all sorted / good to go / sorted (also: someone is comfortably settled)
note: Versatile 'we're set'. Also slang for a person who's financially/romantically settled. Tamil 'set aagiduchu'; the 'settled life' nuance is hard to dub.

actual_meaning: excellent / first-rate / superb
note: English praise phrase used as exclamation. Tamil 'sema/first class' equivalents exist; mostly translatable but the exclamatory delivery matters.

actual_meaning: top-notch / great / excellent (used as praise, not travel)
note: Common South + North praise term. Tamil keeps 'first class' as is; translatable but idiomatic-praise sense must not be read as a ticket grade.

actual_meaning: absolutely top-notch / brilliant
note: Intensifier + praise combo. Translatable in parts but the stacked emphatic flavour ('ekdum') resists clean Tamil mapping.

actual_meaning: absolutely awesome / really nice
note: 'Mast' is slang for great/cool/enjoyable with no single equivalent. Tamil 'sema' is close but loses the laid-back 'mast' vibe.

actual_meaning: it's great / it's cool / it's enjoyable
note: 'Mast' carries pleasure/coolness/laid-backness at once. Tamil 'super/sema iruku' covers part of it; the carefree connotation is dub-lost.

actual_meaning: carefree / chill / bold and unbothered
note: Iconic Mumbai slang for fearless nonchalance. English 'chill/carefree' partial; Tamil borrows it or uses 'tension illa', losing the swagger.

actual_meaning: are we on? / will the plan happen? / will it be fun?
note: 'Scene banega kya' = will the plan come together. Distinct from other 'scene' uses; literal gloss meaningless, needs Tamil 'plan aaguma' rewrite.

actual_meaning: the plan fell through / it got cancelled / mood's spoiled
note: Loan 'off' as 'cancelled/dead'. Three-way code-mix; full rewrite needed in both targets.

actual_meaning: what's going on / what's up
note: Idiomatic 'what's up'. Literal 'what is running' is wrong; Tamil 'enna nadakkudhu' fits in meaning but not the casual texture.

actual_meaning: it's all sorted / situation handled
note: Code-mix of slang 'scene' + English 'sorted'. No literal target equivalent; rewrite as 'ellam set' / 'sab sorted'.

actual_meaning: don't worry / no need to stress (Mumbai construction)
note: Tapori grammar ('-ne ka' infinitive command). English 'tension' loan + dialect syntax; Tamil 'tension edukkadhe' borrows the word, loses the Bambaiya rhythm.

actual_meaning: don't stress / relax
note: 'Take tension' is Indian-English collocation. Tamil keeps 'tension'; literal English 'take tension' is non-native, so dub register varies.

actual_meaning: relax / take it easy
note: 'Maarna' (to hit) used as 'do/have'. Literal 'hit chill' is absurd; Tamil 'chill-a iru' borrows 'chill' but drops the quirky verb.

actual_meaning: depending on the situation / let's read the room first
note: 'Scene dekh ke decide karenge' = decide based on circumstances. Slang 'scene' makes literal Tamil gloss fail; rewrite as 'situation paathu'.

actual_meaning: there's a chance / there's potential (often romantic)
note: Affirmative of 'scope nahi'. Loaned 'scope' = chance; literal English/Tamil 'scope' is too technical for the flirty/optimistic sense.

actual_meaning: we'll figure out a workaround / something will be arranged
note: Combines untranslatable 'jugaad' with vague reassurance 'ho jayega'. Double trouble for dub; full paraphrase needed.

actual_meaning: the job's done / it's sorted
note: Fairly direct ('vela mudinjuduchu' in Tamil). Translatable but used as a satisfied filler-announcement; clipped delivery is the dub concern.

actual_meaning: it'll get done / consider it handled (reassurance)
note: Confident reassurance, often with the vague-timeline 'chalta hai' attitude. Tamil 'vela aaidum' close; the cultural confidence/looseness is dub-sensitive.

actual_meaning: what's up / what do you reckon (tapori greeting)
note: Bambaiya street greeting. Gender-marked, dialectal, untranslatable as a fixed expression; Tamil needs a coined street greeting.

actual_meaning: what's up? / hey, what? (casual, slightly rough address)
note: 'Re' is a low-register vocative particle. Tamil 'enna da/di' is the natural equivalent; literal gloss loses the familiarity marker.

actual_meaning: right? / isn't that so? (seeking agreement)
note: Confirmation tag. Literal 'yes no' is contradictory; Tamil 'illa/dhane' captures the agreement-seeking but the particle stacking differs.

actual_meaning: so, what else is new / fill me in / how've you been (conversational opener)
note: Phatic small-talk prompt, not a literal command. Tamil 'apparam sollu' works but the warm 'catch me up' tone is register-bound.

actual_meaning: forget all that / anyway, moving on (topic-shift)
note: Discourse topic-shift idiom. Literal 'leave all that' is stiff; Tamil 'adhellam vidu' fits semantically, casual flow varies.

actual_meaning: the situation's intense / things are serious right now
note: Slang 'scene' + slang 'heavy'. Double code-mix; literal Tamil rendering fails, rewrite as 'situation serious-a iruku'.

actual_meaning: totally a waste of time / purely for casual fun (and not much else)
note: Intensified 'timepass', often dismissive of a film/event. Untranslatable coinage; Tamil dub keeps 'full timepass'.

actual_meaning: the whole improvise-and-make-do way of doing things
note: Refers to a culture of workarounds. No equivalent; English 'workaround culture' is clinical and loses the affectionate pride in 'jugaad'.

actual_meaning: they have connections / an inside arrangement / a love interest
note: Possessive 'setting' = someone's plug or partner. Ambiguous and slangy; literal gloss meaningless, Tamil needs context-specific rewrite.

actual_meaning: the situation's sorted / the coast is clear / we're good to go
note: Slang 'scene' + 'clear' meaning 'sorted/safe'. Literal Tamil fails; rewrite as 'ellam clear' / 'sab clear'.

actual_meaning: (they/I) picked a fight / got into trouble deliberately
note: Past of 'panga lena'. Untranslatable noun + light verb; Tamil 'scene-a vaanguanga' style rewrite needed.

actual_meaning: we're in trouble now / we're done for
note: Elliptical idiom ('ab lag gayi' = now we're screwed). Literal gloss meaningless; Tamil 'mudinjom' / 'aaiduchu' with despairing tone.

actual_meaning: it'll definitely work out / it's bound to happen, relax
note: Emphatic 'hi' adds certainty to vague reassurance. Tamil 'aagidum-le' close; the particle 'hi' doing emphatic work is hard to mirror.

actual_meaning: if you think about it / actually / on reflection (discourse hedge)
note: Reflective discourse marker, not literal. Tamil 'paathaa' captures the hedge; the contemplative register is subtle for dub.

actual_meaning: something's definitely going on / something fishy here
note: Suspicion idiom built on slang 'scene'. Whole phrase needs rewrite; Tamil 'edho scene iruku' (with borrowed scene) is the natural dub.

actual_meaning: no problem / no drama / it's fine
note: English-flavoured slang for 'no issue'. Tamil 'onnum scene illa' borrows it; literal 'no scene' reads as theatre talk to a non-Indian ear.

actual_meaning: drama after drama / one problem after another
note: Reduplicative slang for cascading trouble. Untranslatable structure; Tamil needs a fresh idiom like 'problem mela problem'.

actual_meaning: settle in / get comfortable / get yourself sorted
note: 'Set' loan verb as imperative for settling down. Tamil 'set aagidu' parallels; the casual life-advice tone is register-bound.

actual_meaning: look at the possibility / check if there's a chance
note: Loan 'scope' = chance/opening. Literal Tamil/Hindi 'scope' too technical; needs 'chance paaru' rewrite for the casual sense.

actual_meaning: you get what I'm saying / I catch your drift (knowing acknowledgement)
note: Often said with a wink to imply unspoken meaning. Tamil 'puriyudhu-le' close; the implicature/wink is the dubbing difficulty.

actual_meaning: exactly! / that's the thing / precisely my point
note: Strong agreement filler. Literal 'that very so' is opaque; Tamil 'adhaan-le' is the natural equivalent, particle work differs.

actual_meaning: that's basically the whole situation / that's all there is to it
note: Wrap-up filler with slang 'scene'. Full rewrite needed; Tamil 'idhuthaan scene' (borrowed) is the natural dub.
